skill.name: file-header-guardian
skill.description: Adds and reviews three-line file header contract comments for source files.
skill.summary: Documents each source file with input, output, and role header comments.
skill.author: Dimon94

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported Ruby or shell backtick execution and weak cryptography, but the cited lines are Markdown metadata and fenced examples. No executable commands, cryptographic code, prompt injection text, network access, or data exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Fenced TypeScript Example
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but line 13 is a Markdown code fence for a TypeScript comment template. No executable shell command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a Markdown fenced code block marker. The surrounding lines contain a comment template, not Ruby or shell execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Closing Markdown Fence
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but line 20 only closes a Markdown code fence. It does not execute code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains only a Markdown fence terminator. There is no command syntax or interpreter context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Fenced Python Example
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but line 23 is a Markdown code fence for a Python docstring template. No executable command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line declares a Markdown example block. The following content is a static docstring template.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Closing Python Example Fence
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but line 30 only closes the Python example block. It has no runtime effect.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a Markdown delimiter. No shell, Ruby, or dynamic execution pattern appears there.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Fenced Shell Comment Example
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but line 33 starts a Markdown bash example containing only comment lines. No shell command is provided.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The block is labeled bash, but the body is only comment syntax for a header template. There is no command invocation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Closing Shell Example Fence
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but line 38 only closes the Markdown bash example. It does not run commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is only a Markdown fence terminator. It cannot execute without an interpreter and contains no command text.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Fenced TypeScript Output Example
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but line 42 starts a Markdown TypeScript example. The block shows a class header comment, not shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line opens a static documentation example. The example contains a class declaration and comments without external command behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alert
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged a weak cryptographic algorithm on line 3, but that line is the skill description metadata. No cryptographic algorithm or hashing operation appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain Chinese description text in front matter. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak cryptographic usage.

content.value_statement: Code files often lack clear ownership and dependency context. This skill prompts Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to add concise headers that document inputs, outputs, and system role.
actual_capabilities: Provides header templates for TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, and shell files.
Defines three standard fields: input, output, and system position.
Prompts review of header accuracy after source file changes.
Reminds users to synchronize file headers with directory CLAUDE.md notes.
Lists common exemptions for configuration, style, generated, and dependency files.
limitations: Does not automatically parse code or generate headers by itself.
Does not enforce headers through a linter or pre-commit hook.
Does not define templates for every programming language.
Relies on the assistant and user to judge whether header content is accurate.

use_cases.title: Standardize New Source Files
use_cases.description: Add consistent contract comments when creating source files in a shared codebase.

use_cases.title: Review Documentation Drift
use_cases.description: Check whether file headers still match dependencies, exports, and module role after edits.

use_cases.title: Maintain Local Architecture Notes
use_cases.description: Keep source file summaries aligned with nearby CLAUDE.md directory documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Basic Header
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one new file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Add the file-header-guardian contract header to this new source file. Fill in input, output, and system position clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Check Header Accuracy
prompt_templates.scenario: Review after editing an existing file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this file using file-header-guardian. Update the header if inputs, outputs, or module role changed.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Across Changed Files
prompt_templates.scenario: Update several source files in one feature branch.
prompt_templates.prompt: For each changed source file, apply file-header-guardian rules. Skip exempt files and report which headers changed.

prompt_templates.title: Align With Directory Documentation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for code and local architecture documentation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit these source files and the directory CLAUDE.md. Make file headers and directory notes consistent without adding unsupported claims.

output_examples.input: A new TypeScript service file with no header.
output_examples.output: The file starts with a short contract comment that names its database dependency, exported service methods, and role in the user module.

output_examples.input: A Python module whose dependencies changed during refactoring.
output_examples.output: The existing docstring header is revised so the input field lists the new dependency and the output field reflects the current public functions.

output_examples.input: A mixed change set containing source files, CSS, and generated declarations.
output_examples.output: Headers are reviewed only for source files. Style files and generated declaration files are identified as exempt.

best_practices: Keep each header field short, factual, and tied to current code behavior.
Update headers in the same change that modifies dependencies or exported behavior.
Check nearby CLAUDE.md documentation when a file role changes.
anti_patterns: Do not add headers to generated files or dependency directories.
Do not describe planned behavior that the file does not currently provide.
Do not let headers become longer than the code they summarize.

faq.question: What files should use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it for source code files where a short contract header improves navigation and review.

faq.question: Which files are exempt?
faq.answer: The skill exempts JSON, YAML, CSS, generated declaration files, and node_modules.

faq.question: Does it execute commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill contains documentation templates and workflow guidance only.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it replace full architecture documentation?
faq.answer: No. It complements directory documentation by keeping each file role visible.

faq.question: How should headers be maintained?
faq.answer: Review the header whenever dependencies, public outputs, or the file role changes.
